## [00:00 - 03:02]

Everyone thinks communication takes years to master. But what if I told you that you could trick your brain to be a better communicator by the end of this week? Because as you type out a text, especially long messages, you get unlimited amount of time to refine, to edit and to polish it. That's great for clarity, but it also means you're not actually practicing your communication in real time. With a voice note on the other hand, you are forced now to get your thoughts out clearly in real time. That's exactly the skill you need in meetings, conversations or when you're presenting on stage. I'll be honest, when I first started doing this, I'd record the same message at least five times before I actually sent it. And my wife, when she saw me do this, she saw me as being crazy. But I saw it as training. Every voice message that I sent became a mini rep to becoming a better communicator. My brain runs at 100 miles per hour. Typing slows me down. With voice notes, I can communicate my full thoughts to my team, my family, in seconds as opposed to minutes. And two, tone and connection. A text can't carry tone and can be misinterpreted in so many different ways. But my voice, people can hear the sincerity, they can hear the energy, they can hear the intent. This is with your mouth, not with your eyes. The first drill is called the E-O drill. E-O-E-O-E-O-E-O When you alternate between the E and the O in an exaggerated way, this stretches the muscles around your mouth and tongue, improving clarity and articulation. Exercise number two, lip flutters, aka the classic... D-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R-R Loosen up your lips and your vocal mask in general, which allows your voice to flow much more freely. And to the next tip, every single time you take a phone call, stand up. Most people take calls slouched at their desk or leaning back in the chair. But the problem is when you do this, your body compresses your lungs and tightens your diaphragm, which then flattens your ability to speak with energy. Whenever I get a phone call, I always make a habit to stand up, immediately go for a walk as I take the call, and it has three powerful benefits. Number one, your voice gets stronger. Singers always sing and rehearse on their feet. Standing opens up your chest, frees up your diaphragm, which allows you to project your voice with more power, with more clarity and with more resonance. Number two, sharper thinking. Research shows that posture influences cognition. Sitting slumped can make your brain sluggish, where standing keeps your blood flowing and primes you to think and respond faster and smarter. Number three, you sound way more energetic. Energy travels through your voice, and when you stand, you naturally use your hand gestures more, and when you move your body, it moves your voice.
